response.description_long:
Commercial buildings heat and cool empty floors on schedules programmed years ago, wasting energy because nobody has time to retell the building management system every season. Gridmaple connects to existing BMS platforms over BACnet — no new sensors, no hardware retrofit — and uses machine learning to forecast occupancy and weather for each zone, then adjusts HVAC schedules and setpoints every 15 minutes. The software loads the building's utility tariff and optimizes against dollars, not just kilowatt-hours, shifting load away from peak pricing and demand charges. Facilities teams set guardrails for temperature bands, equipment cycling, and blackout periods; every adjustment is logged, reversible, and accompanied by a daily digest explaining what changed and why. Gridmaple claims energy savings of 15 to 30 percent on HVAC, verified against utility meters using weather-normalized M&V, and its reports support utility rebate applications and emissions disclosures.

The product targets owners and operators of office, retail, and healthcare portfolios — from a single 80,000-square-foot building to 200 sites. Office landlords use it to hit energy targets in leases and answer tenant sustainability questionnaires; retail portfolios standardize optimization across hundreds of locations with no on-site facilities staff; healthcare operators run it on administrative and outpatient buildings, excluding clinical spaces by guardrail. The company also serves municipal and university estates. Gridmaple's business model is a flat subscription per building per year, sized by floor area, including onboarding, M&V reporting, and rate-tariff updates — no percentage-of-savings contracts.

Gridmaple was founded in 2021 in New York City by Peter Voss and Miguel Santos, controls engineers with a combined twenty years of programming building controls, and Hannah Cole, a former utility tariff analyst. The team of 52 spans engineering, building science, and customer operations. The founding insight is that the energy-saving opportunity in commercial HVAC is not new hardware but continuous, software-driven optimization that applies controls depth and tariff literacy automatically, rather than relying on occasional site visits from commissioning engineers. The company’s approach has been cited in commercial real estate sustainability coverage as a counterpoint to modeled-estimate marketing, and utility program managers in three states accept Gridmaple's M&V package as rebate documentation without supplemental metering.
